texts.label: text
texts.orig: A bstract . Hopf algebras, most generally in a semisimple abelian symmetric monoidal category, are here supposed to be commutative but not to be of finitetype, and their (equivariant) smoothness are discussed. Given a Hopf algebra H in a category such as above, it is proved that the following are equivalent: (i) H is smooth as an algebra; (ii) H is smooth as an H -comodule algebra; (iii) the product morphism S 2 H ( H + ) → H + defined on the 2nd symmetric power is monic. Working over a field k of characteristic zero, we prove: (1) every ordinary Hopf algebra, i.e., such in the category Vec of vector spaces, satisfies the equivalent conditions (i)-(iii) and some others; (2) every Hopf algebra in the category sVec of super-vector spaces has a certain property that is stronger than (i). In the case where char k = p > 0, there are shown weaker properties of ordinary Hopf algebras and of Hopf algebras in sVec or in the ind-completion Ver ind p of the Verlinde category.

texts.orig: This paper discusses smoothness of commutative Hopf algebras not necessarily of finite type, which include commutative Hopf-algebra objects in general categories with appropriate properties. We thus say 'of finite type' for 'finitely generated'. We have three main theorems which are explicitly formulated in the following subsections.
texts.text: This paper discusses smoothness of commutative Hopf algebras not necessarily of finite type, which include commutative Hopf-algebra objects in general categories with appropriate properties. We thus say 'of finite type' for 'finitely generated'. We have three main theorems which are explicitly formulated in the following subsections.

texts.orig: An algebra is said to be smooth (over k ) [30, Section 9.3.1], if given an algebra R and a nilpotent ideal I of R , every algebra map f : A → R / I can lift to some algebra map g : A → R , i.e., the g , composed with the natural projection R → R / I , turns into f . It is known that in the case where A is Noetherian, the condition is equivalent to saying that A is geometrically regular, i.e., it remains regular after base extension to the algebraic closure of k . The definition above is suited when we discuss, as we will do, smoothness of algebras not necessarily of finite type. As another advantage the definition, which concerns algebras in the symmetric monoidal category Vec of vector spaces, can directly extend to those in general categories with appropriate properties. For example, given a Hopf algebra H , we can (and we do) discuss smoothness of H-comodule algebras , i.e., algebras in the symmetric monoidal category of (right or left) H -comodules. texts.orig: 1.3. Background around the equivariant smoothness. The notion of H-crossed products , where H is a not necessary commutative ordinary Hopf algebra, was introduced by Blattner et al. [4]; it generalizes group crossed products, for which H is a group algebra as a special case. To be more explicit, an H -crossed product, R /multicloseright σ H , over a (not necessarily commutative) algebra R is constructed on the H -comodule R ⊗ H , subject to an action (in a weak sense) by H on R and a Hopf 2-cocycle σ : H ⊗ H → R . As was shown by Doi et al. [9], the H -crossed products are intrinsically characterized as H-cleft extensions , i.e., H -comodule algebras with invertible (with respect to the convolution-product) H -colinear map from H . The second-named author [12] pointed out (in fact, in the dual situation) that if the R has an augmentation R → k with square-zero kernel, the augmented H -cleft extensions over R are classified by the 2nd Hochschild cohomologies. As a result it was essentially proved by [12, Theorem 4.1] (see also [16, Theorem 1.2, Proposition 1.10]) that if H is smooth, then it is H -smooth in the present words; the converse holds, as well, but this is rather easy, as will be seen in Proposition 2.9 (modified into the non-commutative situation); see also [17, Proposition 3.19]. The last mentioned result of [12] was re-proved by Ardizonni et al. [1, Theorem 5.9 a)] based on categorical observation using the notion of (co)separability. We reach cohomologies directly (not through augmented cleft extensions), and deduce a key implication, (a) ⇒ (d), of Theorem 1.3 from an isomorphism of the relevant cohomologies; see Proposition 2.5.

texts.orig: We will show that Hopf algebras, which are commutative by assumption, in these categories have some properties stronger than smoothness. The familiar words '(Hopf) superalgebras' meaning (Hopf) algebras in sVec will not be used in this paper. Algebras of sVec in char k = 2 are supposed to satisfy the assumption that every even element is central and every odd element is square-zero, which is stronger than the (super-)commutativity. For Ver ind p , we assume that k is an algebraically closed field of characteristic p > 0. Since Ver ind 2 = Vec and Ver ind 3 = sVec , as is shown in [21, Example 3.2], we assume p ≥ 5, in addition. Then, Ver ind p includes properly sVec as a symmetric monoidal full subcategory.
